claims.statement: Title 12 §158.1 establishes the licensing requirements for private process servers in Oklahoma, including age 18+, Oklahoma residency for 6+ months, county residency for 30+ days, good moral character, a $5,000 bond, and a $150 statewide license fee.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 158.1 - Private process servers - Licensing - Qualifications - Fees - Hearing - Notice - Protests - Proof of service - Revocation of license - List of licensees - Statewide registry
claims.quote: B. Any person who is: 1. Eighteen (18) years of age or older; 2. Of good moral character; 3. Found ethically and mentally fit; 4. A resident of the State of Oklahoma for a period of not less than six (6) months; and 5. A resident of the county or judicial administrative district in which the application is submitted for a period of not less than thirty (30) days, may obtain a license by filing an application with the court clerk on a verified form... C. The applicant filing for a license to serve process anywhere in this state shall pay a license fee of One Hundred Fifty Dollars ($150.00)... upon executing bond running to the State of Oklahoma in the amount of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000.00) for faithful performance of his or her duties...

claims.statement: Title 12 §158.1 requires proof of service of process to be shown by affidavit as provided by subsection G of Section 2004.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 158.1(G) - Proof of service of process
claims.quote: G. Proof of service of process shall be shown by affidavit as provided for by subsection G of Section 2004 of this title.

claims.statement: Title 12 §158.1 makes it a misdemeanor for any person to knowingly and willfully serve process in Oklahoma without a process server license issued by the State of Oklahoma.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 158.1(I) - Unlicensed service misdemeanor
claims.quote: I. Any person who knowingly and willfully serves process in Oklahoma without a process server license issued by the State of Oklahoma or who holds himself or herself out to be a process server licensed by the State of Oklahoma when the person is not licensed as such shall, upon conviction, be guilty of a misdemeanor.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(A) requires the clerk to forthwith issue a summons upon filing of the petition. Separate or additional summons shall issue against any defendants upon request of the plaintiff.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(A) - Summons: Issuance
claims.quote: A. SUMMONS: ISSUANCE. Upon filing of the petition, the clerk shall forthwith issue a summons. Upon request of the plaintiff separate or additional summons shall issue against any defendants.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(C)(1)(a) provides that process shall be served by a sheriff or deputy sheriff, a person licensed to make service of process in civil cases, or a person specially appointed for that purpose. The court shall freely make special appointments.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(C)(1)(a) - By Whom Served: Personal Delivery
claims.quote: a. At the election of the plaintiff, process, other than a subpoena, shall be served by a sheriff or deputy sheriff, a person licensed to make service of process in civil cases or a person specially appointed for that purpose. The court shall freely make special appointments to serve all process, other than a subpoena, under this paragraph.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(C)(1)(c)(1) provides that service on an individual is made by delivering a copy of the summons and petition personally, or by leaving copies at the person's dwelling house with someone age 15+ residing therein.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(C)(1)(c)(1) - Service on individuals
claims.quote: (1) upon an individual other than an infant who is less than fifteen (15) years of age or an incompetent person, by delivering a copy of the summons and of the petition personally or by leaving copies thereof at the person's dwelling house or usual place of abode with some person then residing therein who is fifteen (15) years of age or older...

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(C)(2) provides that service by mail shall be accomplished by certified mail, return receipt requested and delivery restricted to the addressee. Service by mail is effective on the date of receipt or, if refused, on the date of refusal.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(C)(2) - Service by mail
claims.quote: a. At the election of the plaintiff, a summons and petition may be served by mail... Service by mail shall be effective on the date of receipt or if refused, on the date of refusal... b. Service by mail shall be accomplished by mailing a copy of the summons and petition by certified mail, return receipt requested and delivery restricted to the addressee.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(C)(3) provides that service of summons upon a named defendant may be made by publication when verified that with due diligence service cannot be made by any other method. Publication must run one day a week for three consecutive weeks.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(C)(3) - Service by publication
claims.quote: a. Service of summons upon a named defendant may be made by publication when it is stated in the petition, verified by the plaintiff or the plaintiff's attorney or in a separate affidavit... that with due diligence service cannot be made upon the defendant by any other method. c. Service pursuant to this paragraph shall be made by publication of a notice, signed by the court clerk, one (1) day a week for three (3) consecutive weeks in a newspaper authorized by law to publish legal notices...

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(D) requires the summons and petition to be served together. If a summons and petition are served by personal delivery, the person serving shall state on the copy left with the person served the date that service is made.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(D) - Summons and petition served together
claims.quote: D. SUMMONS AND PETITION. The summons and petition shall be served together. The plaintiff shall furnish the person making service with such copies as are necessary... If a summons and petition are served by personal delivery, the person serving the summons shall state on the copy that is left with the person served the date that service is made.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(G)(2) requires the person serving process to make proof of service promptly. If service is made by a person other than a sheriff or deputy sheriff, the licensed process server shall make affidavit thereof. The return shall set forth the county of issuance, name of the person served, and date, place, and method of service.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(G) - Return
claims.quote: 2. When process has been served by a sheriff or deputy sheriff and return thereof is filed in the office of the court clerk, a copy of the return shall be sent by the court clerk to the plaintiff's attorney within three (3) days after the return is filed. If service is made by a person other than a sheriff or deputy sheriff, the licensed process server shall make affidavit thereof. The return shall set forth the county of issuance, the name of the person served and the date, place and method of service.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004(I) establishes a 180-day time limit for service of process. If service is not made within 180 days after filing and the plaintiff has not shown good cause, the action shall be deemed dismissed as to that defendant without prejudice.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(I) - Summons: Time Limit for Service
claims.quote: I. SUMMONS: TIME LIMIT FOR SERVICE. If service of process is not made upon a defendant within one hundred eighty (180) days after the filing of the petition and the plaintiff has not shown good cause why such service was not made within that period, the action shall be deemed dismissed as to that defendant without prejudice... Upon application of a defendant not timely served, the court shall enter an order dismissing the action as to that defendant. The court shall enter a dismissal order of an action within two hundred (200) days after the filing of the action in which no service has been made on any defendant...

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004.1(B)(1) provides that service of a subpoena may be accomplished by any person who is 18 years of age or older. Service by mail may be accomplished by certified mail with return receipt requested and delivery restricted.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004.1(B) - Service of subpoena
claims.quote: B. 1. SERVICE. Service of a subpoena upon a person named therein shall be made by delivering or mailing a copy thereof to such person and, if the person's attendance is demanded, by tendering to that person the fees for one (1) day's attendance and the mileage allowed by law. Service of a subpoena may be accomplished by any person who is eighteen (18) years of age or older... 2. Service of a subpoena by mail may be accomplished by mailing a copy thereof by certified mail with return receipt requested and delivery restricted to the person named in the subpoena.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2004.3 allows alternate delivery methods using commercial courier service, overnight delivery service, or other reliable personal delivery service, evidenced by a written or electronic receipt signed by the addressee.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004.3 - Alternate delivery methods for copy of process, papers
claims.quote: A. In lieu of mailing a copy of process or other papers by certified mail, return receipt requested and delivery restricted to the addressee as required or allowed by this title, a party or attorney may send the same by commercial courier service, overnight delivery service, or other reliable personal delivery service to the party addressed, in each instance evidenced by a written or electronic receipt signed by the addressee showing to whom delivered, date of delivery, address where delivered, and person or entity effecting delivery.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2005(A) requires service of every pleading subsequent to the original petition, every paper relating to discovery, every written motion other than ex parte, and every written notice upon each of the parties.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005(A) - Service: When Required
claims.quote: A. SERVICE: WHEN REQUIRED. Except as otherwise provided in this title, every order required by its terms to be served, every pleading subsequent to the original petition unless the court otherwise orders because of numerous defendants, every paper relating to discovery required to be served upon a party or any other person unless the court otherwise orders, every written motion other than one which may be heard ex parte, and every written notice, appearance, demand, offer of judgment, and similar paper shall be served upon each of the parties.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2005(B) provides that service upon an attorney or party shall be made by delivering a copy, mailing it, sending by third-party commercial carrier, or by electronic means if written consent has been given. Delivery includes handing it to the attorney/party, leaving it at their office, or leaving it at their dwelling with someone age 15+.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005(B) - Service: How Made
claims.quote: B. SERVICE: HOW MADE. Whenever pursuant to this act service is required or permitted to be made upon a party represented by an attorney the service shall be made upon the attorney unless service directly upon the party is ordered by the court... Service upon the attorney or upon a party shall be made by delivering a copy to the attorney or the party or by mailing it or sending it by third-party commercial carrier for delivery within three (3) calendar days... or by electronic means if the attorney or party consents in writing... Delivery of a copy within this section means: 1. Handing it to the attorney or to the party; or 2. Leaving it at the office of the attorney or the party with the attorney's or party's clerk or other person in charge thereof; or 3. If there is no one in charge, leaving it in a conspicuous place therein; or 4. If the office is closed or the person to be served has no office, leaving it at his or her dwelling house or usual place of abode with some person residing therein who is fifteen (15) years of age or older.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2005(D) requires all papers after the petition to be filed with the court either before service or within a reasonable time thereafter. All papers filed shall include a statement setting forth the names of persons served and the date, place, and method of service.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005(D) - Filing
claims.quote: D. FILING. All papers after the petition required to be served upon a party shall be filed with the court either before service or within a reasonable time thereafter... All papers filed with the court shall include a statement setting forth the names of the persons served and the date, place, and method of service.

claims.statement: Title 12 §2005.1 requires all postjudgment motions pertaining to divorce proceedings to be served in accordance with subsection C of Section 2004 (service of summons methods).
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005.1 - Service of postjudgment motions in divorce actions
claims.quote: SERVICE OF POSTJUDGMENT MOTIONS IN DIVORCE ACTIONS. All postjudgment motions pertaining to divorce proceedings shall be served in accordance with subsection C of Section 2004 of Title 12 of the Oklahoma Statutes.

claims.statement: Title 22 §711 provides that a peace officer must serve any subpoena delivered to him for service and make a written return, but a subpoena may be served by any other person.
claims.source_title: 22 OK Stat § 711 - Service of subpoena by whom - Return
claims.quote: §22-711. Service of subpoena by whom-Return. A peace officer must serve in his county, city, town or village, as the case may be, any subpoena delivered to him for service, either on the part of the state or of the defendant, and must make a written return of the service, subscribed by him, stating the time and place of service without delay. A subpoena may, however, be served by any other person.

claims.statement: Title 22 §712 provides that service of subpoenas for witnesses in criminal actions shall be made in the same manner as in civil actions pursuant to Section 2004.1 of Title 12. Service may be accomplished by any person age 18+, and by certified mail with return receipt requested.
claims.source_title: 22 OK Stat § 712 - Service, manner of - Cost (as amended by HB 1563, 2025)
claims.quote: Section 712. A. Service of subpoenas for witnesses in criminal actions in the district courts of this state a subpoena upon a person named therein shall be made in the same manner as in civil actions pursuant to Section 2004.1 of Title 12 of the Oklahoma Statutes by delivering or mailing a copy thereof to such person and, if the attendance of the person is demanded, by tendering to that person the fees for one (1) day of attendance and the mileage allowed by law. Service of a subpoena may be accomplished by any person who is eighteen (18) years of age or older. B. Service of a subpoena by mail may be accomplished by mailing a copy by certified mail with return receipt requested and delivery restricted to the person named in the subpoena.

claims.statement: Title 41 §111(E) specifies how written notice to terminate tenancy must be served: personally on the tenant; if tenant cannot be located, on any family member over age 12 residing with the tenant; if neither can be found, posted conspicuously plus mailed by certified mail.
claims.source_title: 41 OK Stat § 111(E) - Termination of tenancy - Notice service
claims.quote: E. The written notice, required by the Oklahoma Residential Landlord and Tenant Act, to terminate any tenancy shall be served on the tenant or landlord personally unless otherwise specified by law. If the tenant cannot be located, service shall be made by delivering the notice to any family member of such tenant over the age of twelve (12) years residing with the tenant. If service cannot be made on the tenant personally or on such family member, notice shall be posted at a conspicuous place on the dwelling unit of the tenant. If the notice is posted, a copy of such notice shall be mailed to the tenant by certified mail or by mailing such notice through the Firm Mailing Book for Accountable Mail as provided by the United States Post Office.

claims.statement: Title 41 §132(C) and (D) allow immediate termination without advance notice for criminal activity threatening health/safety or drug-related criminal activity, permitting the landlord to immediately file a forcible entry and detainer action.
claims.source_title: 41 OK Stat § 132(C)-(D) - Immediate termination for criminal activity
claims.quote: C. Notwithstanding other provisions of this section, if there is a noncompliance by the tenant with the rental agreement or with any of the provisions of Section 127 of this title, which noncompliance causes or threatens to cause imminent and irremediable harm to the premises or to any person... the landlord may terminate the rental agreement by immediately filing a forcible entry and detainer action. D. Any criminal activity that threatens the health, safety or right of peaceful enjoyment of the premises by other tenants... and any drug-related criminal activity on or near the premises... shall be grounds for immediate termination of the lease.

claims.statement: Title 12 §1148.4 requires the summons in forcible entry and detainer actions to command the defendant to appear for trial not less than 5 days nor more than 10 days from the date the summons is issued (15 days for certain actions under 2025 amendments).
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 1148.4 - FED Summons issuance and return
claims.quote: The summons shall be issued and returned as in other cases, except that it shall command the sheriff, or other person serving it, to summon the defendant to appear for trial at the time and place specified therein, which time shall be not less than five (5) days nor more than ten (10) days from the date that the summons is issued...

claims.statement: Title 12 §1148.5 requires FED summons service at least 3 days before trial (7 days under 2025 SB 128 for non-emergency actions), by leaving a copy with someone over 15 residing on the premises, or by certified mail if personal service cannot be made.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 1148.5 - FED Service of summons
claims.quote: The summons may be served as in other cases except that such service shall be at least three (3) days before the day of trial... and it may also be served by leaving a copy thereof with some person over fifteen (15) years of age, residing on the premises, at least three (3) days before the day of trial; or, if service cannot be made by the exercise of reasonable diligence on the tenant or on any person over the age of fifteen (15) years residing on the premises, the same may be served by certified mail with return receipt postmarked at least three (3) days before the date of trial.

claims.statement: Title 12 §1148.5A allows constructive service of FED summons by posting conspicuously on the building and mailing by certified mail to the last-known address if personal service cannot be made. Such service only confers jurisdiction for restoration of possession, not money judgment.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 1148.5A - Constructive service of summons in FED
claims.quote: If, in the exercise of reasonable diligence, service cannot be made upon the defendant personally nor upon any person residing upon the premises over fifteen (15) years of age, then in lieu of service by certified mail, service may be obtained... by the sheriff's posting or by private process service posting of said the summons conspicuously on the building on the premises... Such service shall confer no jurisdiction upon the court to render any judgment against the defendant for the payment of money nor for any relief other than the restoration of possession of the premises to the claimant, unless the defendant appears at trial.

claims.statement: Title 43 §110 provides that upon filing of a petition for dissolution and upon personal service of the petition and summons on the respondent, or upon waiver and acceptance of service, an automatic temporary injunction shall be in effect against both parties.
claims.source_title: 43 OK Stat § 110 - Automatic temporary injunction - Temporary orders
claims.quote: A. 1. Except as otherwise provided by this subsection, upon the filing of a petition for dissolution of marriage, annulment of a marriage or legal separation by the petitioner and upon personal service of the petition and summons on the respondent, or upon waiver and acceptance of service by the respondent, an automatic temporary injunction shall be in effect against both parties pursuant to the provisions of this section...

claims.statement: Title 43 §110(B)(1) provides that either party may request temporary orders. All applications for temporary orders shall be served on the other party in any manner provided for in the Rules of Civil Procedure, with at least 5 days' notice of hearing.
claims.source_title: 43 OK Stat § 110(B) - Temporary orders service
claims.quote: B. After a petition has been filed in an action for dissolution of marriage or legal separation either party may request the court to issue: 1. A temporary order... All applications for temporary orders shall set forth the factual basis for the application and shall be verified by the party seeking relief. The application and a notice of hearing shall be served on the other party in any manner provided for in the Rules of Civil Procedure. The court shall not issue a temporary order until at least five (5) days' notice of hearing is given to the other party.

claims.statement: Title 12 §1751 defines small claims as actions for recovery of money up to $10,000 based on contract or tort (excluding libel/slander), replevin actions for property valued up to $10,000, and interpleader actions.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 1751 - Suits authorized under small claims procedure
claims.quote: A. The following suits may be brought under the small claims procedure: 1. Actions for the recovery of money based on contract or tort, including subrogation claims, but excluding libel or slander, in which the amount sought to be recovered, exclusive of attorney fees and other court costs, does not exceed Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00); 2. Actions to replevy personal property the value of which does not exceed Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00)...

claims.statement: Title 12 §1764 establishes small claims filing fees: $45 for actions of $5,000 or less; for actions over $5,000 the filing fees are the same as district court. Service by sheriff or licensed private process server has usual fees taxed as costs.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 1764 - Small Claims Procedure Act Fees
claims.quote: A fee of Forty-five Dollars ($45.00) shall be charged and collected for the filing of the affidavit for the commencement of any action for an amount of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000.00) or less. Any action in excess of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000.00) shall be subject to the filing fees provided in Title 28 of the Oklahoma Statutes for the same kind of action as filed in district court... If the affidavit and order are served by the sheriff or a licensed private process server, the court clerk shall collect the usual fee for the sheriff, which shall be taxed as costs in the case. The fee paid to a licensed private process server, as approved by the court, shall be taxed as additional costs in the case.

claims.statement: HB 1129 (2025 session) proposes to increase the small claims filing fee from $45 to $85 for cases involving $5,000 or less.
claims.source_title: OK HB1129 - Small claims filing fee increase (2025)
claims.quote: This bill amends Oklahoma law by increasing the filing fee for small claims court actions from $45 to $85 for cases involving $5,000 or less.

claims.statement: Service of process in small claims court can be achieved through personal service by sheriff ($50 in-county, $60 out-of-county), certified mail through clerk's office ($10), or private process server.
claims.source_title: Creek County Small Claims - Service methods and fees
claims.quote: The forms of service are sheriff, which is $50.00 in-county and $60.00 out of county; certified mail, which is $10.00, if done by the clerk's office; and private process server.

claims.statement: Oklahoma process server license application fee is $150 for statewide authority, plus a $5,000 bond. Annual renewal fee is $15 per three-year renewal period.
claims.source_title: McCurtain County - Private Process Server Licensing Fees
claims.quote: New Process Server - Statewide $364.14 - Good for one (1) year... Renewal Process Server - Statewide $229.14 - Good for three (3) years. Statute Reference: 12 O.S. §158.1

claims.statement: The Administrative Office of the Courts provides process server licensing forms on OSCN.net including: Application, Public Notice, Proof of Posting, Order Granting Application, and the License itself.
claims.source_title: OSCN Process Server Forms - AOC Forms
claims.quote: https://www.oscn.net/static/forms/aoc_forms/processserver.asp. Forms Needed: Application, Public Notice, Proof of Posting, Order Granting Application for Statewide Private Process Server License, Statewide Private Process Server License

claims.statement: The Oklahoma Supreme Court Network (OSCN) provides court forms including Entry of Appearance, Notice of Change of Address, In Forma Pauperis Affidavit, and other civil practice forms at oksc.oscn.net/forms/.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Supreme Court Network - Court Forms
claims.quote: Forms: 1. Entry of Appearance. 2. Entry of Appearance - Pro se. 3. Notice of Change of Address. 4. In Forma Pauperis Affidavit. 4A. Affidavit of intent to remit cost deposit via credit or debit card...

claims.statement: 22 O.S. §60.25 prohibits filing fees, service of process fees and attorney fee awards upon plaintiffs seeking protective orders unless the court finds the petition was filed frivolously.
claims.source_title: 22 OK Stat § 60.25 - Fees not required for protective orders
claims.quote: In any action seeking a protective order, no filing fee, service of process fee or attorney fees shall be assessed to the plaintiff, unless the court finds that the petition was filed frivolously.

claims.statement: The Oklahoma Pleading Code (Title 12 §§2001-2101) incorporates the Rules of Civil Procedure into statute, including provisions for service of process, subpoenas, pleadings, and time computation.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2001 - Scope of the Oklahoma Pleading Code
claims.quote: § 2001. Scope of the Oklahoma Pleading Code... § 2004. Process... § 2004.1. Subpoena... § 2005. Service and Filing of Pleadings and Other Papers... § 2006. Time...

claims.statement: Service by mail for pleadings (other than summons and original petition) is complete upon mailing. Service by commercial carrier is complete upon delivery to the carrier. Service by electronic means is complete upon transmission.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005(B) - Service completion timing
claims.quote: Except for service of the summons and the original petition, service by mail is complete upon mailing, service by commercial carrier is complete upon delivery to the commercial carrier, and service by electronic means is complete upon transmission, unless the party making service is notified that the copy or paper served was not received by the party served.

claims.statement: No prisoner in any jail, Department of Corrections facility, private prison, or parolee or probationer shall be appointed by any court to serve process on any defendant, party or witness.
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004(C)(7) - No service by prisoner
claims.quote: 7. NO SERVICE BY PRISONER. No prisoner in any jail, Department of Corrections facility, private prison, or parolee or probationer under supervision of the Department of Corrections shall be appointed by any court to serve process on any defendant, party or witness.

claims.statement: The 2025 amendments to the FED statutes (SB 128) extend the trial date to 5-15 days after summons issuance, and extend service time to 7 days before trial (from 3 days), except for emergency/criminal actions which remain at 3 days/5-10 days.
claims.source_title: SB 128 (2025) - Forcible Entry and Detainer summons amendments
claims.quote: Section 1148.4... which time shall be not less than five (5) days nor more than ten (10) days nor more than fifteen (15) days from the date that the summons is issued; provided, however, a trial for an action pursuant to subsection C or D of Section 132 of Title 41... shall be not less than five (5) days nor more than ten (10) days... Section 1148.5... such service shall be at least three (3) seven (7) days before the day of trial...

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 2 requires that proof of service state the name of the person served and the date, place, and method of service
claims.source_title: Rule 2. Service and Proof of Service - Rules for District Courts of Oklahoma
claims.quote: a. The person serving the process shall state in his proof the name of the person served and the date, place and method of service. See 12 O.S. § 2004G.

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 2 requires defendants to file responsive pleadings within 20 days after being served with process
claims.source_title: IN RE AMENDMENTS TO OKLAHOMA SUPREME COURT RULES, 2013 OK 68
claims.quote: b. (i) Defendants, third-party defendants and persons who are joined as parties to an action shall file their responsive pleading with the court clerk and serve copies on all opposing parties within 20 days after being served with process unless the time is extended by the service and filing of a motion or by the entry of an appearance

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 2 allows service of pleadings and motions by any person 18 years or older, with proof by attorney certificate or affidavit
claims.source_title: IN RE AMENDMENTS TO OKLAHOMA SUPREME COURT RULES, 2013 OK 68
claims.quote: (iii) Where service of a pleading, motion or other instrument is made by delivery, the delivery shall be performed by any person who is 18 years of age or older. Proof of service, whether made by delivery or mail, shall be made by the certificate of an attorney of record, or if made by any other person, by the affidavit of such person.

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 2.1 authorizes electronic filing and electronic service as authorized by the Rules for Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts
claims.source_title: Rule 2.1. Electronic Filing, Service, and Signature
claims.quote: a. Electronic Filing. Whenever these rules require a pleading, motion, document, or other instrument to be filed or delivered to the court clerk, such requirement may be satisfied by electronic filing as authorized by the Rules for Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts, and any other applicable statute or court rule.

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 2.1(b) provides that the term 'mail' includes transmission by electronic mail when authorized by the Rules for Electronic Filing
claims.source_title: Rule 2.1. Electronic Filing, Service, and Signature
claims.quote: b. Electronic Service. Whenever these rules require a pleading, motion, document or other instrument to be served, mailed, transmitted, or issued, such requirement may be satisfied by electronic methods as authorized by the Rules for Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts... As used in these rules, the term 'mail' 'mailing' or 'mailed' shall include transmission by electronic mail, when authorized by the Rules for Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 2.1(c)(2) allows notarized documents and documents signed under penalty of perjury to be filed electronically, with the filer retaining the original
claims.source_title: Rule 2.1. Electronic Filing, Service, and Signature
claims.quote: 2. Pleadings, motions, affidavits, waivers, or other instruments which are signed under penalty of perjury, or notarized may be filed electronically, pursuant to the Rules for Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts or other applicable court rule or statute... The filer shall retain the original document, or other evidence of the original signature(s), for future production.

claims.statement: Oklahoma District Court Rule 3 states that objections to jurisdiction over the person, issuance or service of summons, or venue are waived if a party asks for affirmative relief in a permissive counterclaim, cross-claim, or third-party petition
claims.source_title: Rule 3. Objections to Service and Venue - Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure (mirroring state rule)
claims.quote: Objections to the jurisdiction of the court over the person, to the issuance or service of the summons or to the venue of the action are waived and a party submits himself to the jurisdiction of the court if he asks for affirmative relief on a claim which is asserted in a permissive counterclaim, in a cross-claim, or in a third-party petition. The assertion of a compulsory counterclaim against a plaintiff does not waive any of the above objections.

claims.statement: Oklahoma Supreme Court Rule 1.4(g) requires proof of service by certificate endorsed on the filing for all documents filed with the Supreme Court or Court of Civil Appeals
claims.source_title: Rule 1.4. Supreme Court Clerk, Guidelines for Filers, Filings
claims.quote: (1) By Parties. Service of all documents filed with the Supreme Court or Court of Civil Appeals shall be made in the manner provided in 12 O.S. § 2005(B), or as provided in the Rules for Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts. Proof of service may be by a certificate of service endorsed on the filing. The Court, a Justice thereof, or a Referee of the Supreme Court may require other methods of service and proof of service.

claims.statement: Electronic Filing Rule 8(a) authorizes electronic service only for documents permitted to be served by first-class mail, express mail, overnight delivery, or facsimile - not for service of process or documents requiring personal service
claims.source_title: Rule 8. Electronic Service (E-Service) - Rules for Electronic Filing (Pilot Courts)
claims.quote: a. Authorization for Electronic Service. Where any statute or court rule requires service of a Document on another person, that requirement may be accomplished by Electronic Service. Electronic Service of Documents is limited to those Documents permitted to be served by first-class mail, express mail, overnight delivery, or facsimile transmission. Service of process and service of Documents that require personal service as a matter of law may not be accomplished electronically.

claims.statement: Electronic Filing Rule 8(b) states that e-service is effective upon transmission to the Designated Case-Specific Email Address, but automatic OUCMS notices do not constitute electronic service
claims.source_title: Rule 8. Electronic Service (E-Service) - Rules for Electronic Filing (Pilot Courts)
claims.quote: E-Service is effective upon the transmission of the Document by the Filer to the Designated Case-Specific Email Address of the person being served... Automatic notices generated by the OUCMS regarding docket activity or case filings shall not constitute Electronic Service upon any party.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(C)(1)(a) provides for personal service by delivering a copy of summons and petition personally, or by leaving copies at the dwelling house with a person 15+ years old residing therein (substituted service)
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: Service shall be made as follows: (1) upon an individual other than an infant who is less than fifteen (15) years of age or an incompetent person, by delivering a copy of the summons and of the petition personally or by leaving copies thereof at the person's dwelling house or usual place of abode with some person then residing therein who is fifteen (15) years of age or older, by delivering a copy of the summons and of the petition to an agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(G)(1) requires proof of service to be made promptly and within the time during which the person served must respond
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: G. RETURN. 1. The person serving the process shall make proof of service thereof to the court promptly and in any event within the time during which the person served must respond to the process, but the failure to make proof of service does not affect the validity of the service.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(G)(2) requires licensed process servers to make affidavit of service, setting forth the county of issuance, name of person served, and date, place and method of service
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: If service is made by a person other than a sheriff or deputy sheriff, the licensed process server shall make affidavit thereof. The return shall set forth the county of issuance, the name of the person served and the date, place and method of service.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(I) establishes a 180-day deadline for service of process after filing the petition
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: I. SUMMONS: TIME LIMIT FOR SERVICE. If service of process is not made upon a defendant within one hundred eighty (180) days after the filing of the petition and the plaintiff has not shown good cause why such service was not made within that period, the action shall be deemed dismissed as to that defendant without prejudice

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2005(B) requires service upon a party's attorney (not the party directly) unless the court orders otherwise, and permits service by electronic means with written consent
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005 (2025) - Service and filing of pleadings and other papers
claims.quote: B. SERVICE: HOW MADE. Whenever pursuant to this act service is required or permitted to be made upon a party represented by an attorney the service shall be made upon the attorney unless service directly upon the party is ordered by the court... Service upon the attorney or upon a party shall be made by delivering a copy to the attorney or the party or by mailing it or sending it by third-party commercial carrier for delivery within three (3) calendar days... or by electronic means if the attorney or party consents in writing to receiving service in a particular case by electronic means

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2005(B) defines electronic means as including facsimile or electronic mail (e-mail)
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005 (2025) - Service and filing of pleadings and other papers
claims.quote: For purposes of this subsection, 'electronic means' includes communications by facsimile or electronic mail through the internet, commonly known as e-mail.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2005(D) requires all filed papers to include a statement of the names of persons served and the date, place, and method of service
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2005 (2025) - Service and filing of pleadings and other papers
claims.quote: D. FILING. All papers after the petition required to be served upon a party shall be filed with the court either before service or within a reasonable time thereafter... All papers filed with the court shall include a statement setting forth the names of the persons served and the date, place, and method of service.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 158.1 establishes private process server licensing requirements: age 18+, good moral character, Oklahoma residency for 6 months, county residency for 30 days, and a $5,000 bond
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 158.1 (2025) - Private process servers - Licensing
claims.quote: B. Any person who is: 1. Eighteen (18) years of age or older; 2. Of good moral character; 3. Found ethically and mentally fit; 4. A resident of the State of Oklahoma for a period of not less than six (6) months; and 5. A resident of the county or judicial administrative district in which the application is submitted for a period of not less than thirty (30) days, may obtain a license by filing an application with the court clerk

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 158.1(C) provides for a statewide license at $150 fee with 3-year renewals, and the license grants authority to serve process in any county in Oklahoma
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 158.1 (2025) - Private process servers - Licensing
claims.quote: C. The applicant filing for a license to serve process anywhere in this state shall pay a license fee of One Hundred Fifty Dollars ($150.00)... The license shall state that the licensee is an officer of the court only for the purpose of service of process. The authority of the licensee shall be statewide.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 158.1(K) requires the Administrative Office of the Courts to maintain a statewide registry of licensed private process servers
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 158.1 (2025) - Private process servers - Licensing
claims.quote: K. No later than January 1, 2013, the Administrative Office of the Courts shall establish and maintain a statewide registry which shall contain a list of licensed private process servers.

claims.statement: Tulsa County Local Court Rule 17 governs private process servers, including licensing procedures, $5,000 bond requirement, and rules of conduct
claims.source_title: Tulsa County District Court Rules - General Rules, Rule 17
claims.quote: RULE 17. Private Process Servers... A licensed process server is authorized to serve process issued by Tulsa County in civil cases anywhere within the State of Oklahoma... The initial fee for Tulsa County licenses is $150, or $265 for statewide licenses... Upon an annual filing of a certified copy of a license... and payment of a filing fee of ten (10) dollars... a licensed process server may serve process in that county

claims.statement: Tulsa County requires designation of the process server on the face of the summons or order, with the server's name and license number
claims.source_title: Tulsa County District Court Rules - Designation of Process Server
claims.quote: Any person using a licensed process server shall designate on the face of the summons or order the name and license number(s) of the process server or servers selected from the Court Clerk's approved list and shall sign the designation.

claims.statement: Cleveland County (21st Judicial District) Local Rule No. 8 requires a certificate of service on all filed motions and pleadings, and requires a Notice of Consent to Accept Service by Email for electronic service
claims.source_title: OFFICIAL COURT RULES OF THE TWENTY-FIRST JUDICIAL DISTRICT - Rule No. 8
claims.quote: B. Parties or attorneys filing motions, pleadings, orders or journal entries after the petition has been filed shall serve copies as provided in 12 O.S. §2005, except as otherwise provided by law. A certificate of service shall be noted on the original instrument filed... C. If a party or attorney consents to service by email, that party or attorney shall file a Notice of Consent to Accept Service by Email.

claims.statement: Cleveland County Local Rule No. 19 requires a Servicemember's affidavit in accordance with the SCRA for all default judgments against individuals
claims.source_title: OFFICIAL COURT RULES OF THE TWENTY-FIRST JUDICIAL DISTRICT - Rule No. 19
claims.quote: When the defaulting party has not made an appearance... the Plaintiff may file a motion for default judgment reciting: 1. The method and date of service; 2. The date proof of service was filed; 3. The date by which the Defendant was required to respond; and 4. What notice of the default was given to the Defendant... Servicemember's affidavit in accordance with the Servicemember's Civil Relief Act of 2003 and Department of Defense Status Report in all civil cases involving individuals

claims.statement: Oklahoma County local rules (as of August 2023) include provisions for a private process server docket and related procedures
claims.source_title: Oklahoma County Local Rules Updated August 2023
claims.quote: Conducts or designates a judge to conduct the private process server docket ... document filed with the Court Clerk, unless otherwise ordered by the Court.

claims.statement: The Oklahoma E-Filing System allows attorneys, private process servers, and state agency representatives to electronically file court documents
claims.source_title: OSCN E-Filing Quick Start Guide
claims.quote: The Oklahoma Electronic Filing System allows Oklahoma attorneys, private process servers, and state agency representatives to efficiently upload and electronically file court documents into existing district court cases through the Internet using a standard web browser.

claims.statement: All e-filed documents must be in text-based PDF format with a 2-inch top margin on the first page for the electronic file stamp
claims.source_title: OSCN E-Filing Quick Start Guide - Document Preparation
claims.quote: All E-Filed documents shall be filed in a text-based PDF format pursuant to the Rules For Electronic Filing in the Oklahoma Courts... The first page of an e-Document should have a top margin of at least two(2) inches to allow for insertion of an electronic file stamp and/or certification.

claims.statement: Documents submitted outside business hours, weekends, or holidays are deemed submitted the next business day
claims.source_title: OSCN E-Filing Quick Start Guide - Filing Deadlines
claims.quote: Any document(s) submitted outside of the district court clerks regular business hours., on a weekend, on a holiday, or during office closures due to inclement weather, will be deemed submitted the next business day.

claims.statement: The E-Filing Rules define Electronic Service (E-Service) as the electronic transmission of E-Documents by email to Registered Users in lieu of serving in traditional paper form
claims.source_title: OKLAHOMA RULES FOR E-FILING IN SELECTED PILOT COURTS, SCAD-2012-36
claims.quote: j. 'Electronic Service' ('E-Service') means the electronic transmission of E-Documents by email to one or more Registered Users in lieu of serving such Documents in traditional paper form.

claims.statement: The E-Filing Rules require a Designated Case-Specific Email Address that serves as the last known address for purposes of service once provided
claims.source_title: OKLAHOMA RULES FOR E-FILING IN SELECTED PILOT COURTS, SCAD-2012-36
claims.quote: 1. 'Designated Case-Specific Email Address' is the primary email address provided by the Registered User in a specific case or matter for E-Service in that specific case or matter... Once a Registered User provides a Designated Case-Specific Email Address in a specific case, that address shall serve as the 'last known address' for purposes of service in that matter

claims.statement: The Oklahoma Supreme Court provides standard forms on OSCN including Entry of Appearance (Form 1), Entry of Appearance Pro Se (Form 2), Notice of Change of Address (Form 3), and In Forma Pauperis Affidavit (Form 4)
claims.source_title: Forms - Oklahoma Supreme Court - OSCN.net
claims.quote: |1. Entry of Appearance.|Word|PDF| |2. Entry of Appearance - Pro se.|Word|PDF| |3. Notice of Change of Address.|Word|PDF| |4. In Forma Pauperis Affidavit.|Word|PDF|

claims.statement: Military affidavits (Non-Military Affidavits) are required under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act before default judgment can be entered, to certify a defendant is not on active military duty
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Non-Military Affidavit Information
claims.quote: The Oklahoma Non-Military Affidavit is a legal document used by petitioners to certify that the respondent in a divorce case is not on active duty in the armed services... This affidavit assures the court that the individual in question is not on active duty, thereby influencing the legal context of family law cases.

claims.statement: The E-Filing Rules include a Form No. 1 for Notice of attachments not electronically filed
claims.source_title: OKLAHOMA RULES FOR E-FILING IN SELECTED PILOT COURTS, SCAD-2012-36 - Form No. 1
claims.quote: A notice regarding the inability to E-File any attachments shall be appended to the end of the principal E-Document and a copy of that notice shall also accompany all copies of the principal Document that are served on the parties and any court officials in the case. See Form No. 1.

claims.statement: OSCN dockets show filing events including 'SUMMONS RETURNED / AFFIDAVIT OF SERVICE' as standard docket entries
claims.source_title: OSCN Case Details - Sample Docket Entry
claims.quote: SUMMONS RETURNED / AFFIDAVIT OF SERVICE. Document Available (1062654963)

claims.statement: Tulsa County process server rules prohibit carrying weapons while serving unless the server has an officer's commission or concealed carry certification
claims.source_title: Tulsa County District Court Rules - Rules Governing Private Process Servers
claims.quote: A process server will never carry any type of gun or weapon unless he or she has an officer's commission or has been certified as required by law to carry a concealed weapon.

claims.statement: Tulsa County process servers must show their licenses and advise the person they are process servers when serving; licenses must be carried at all times while on duty; badges are prohibited
claims.source_title: Tulsa County District Court Rules - Rules Governing Private Process Servers
claims.quote: When serving a person, process servers must show their licenses and advise the person they are process servers. Licenses shall be carried at all times while on duty. The use of any badge is prohibited.

claims.statement: Oklahoma Administrative Code § 340:25-5-190 requires 'diligent efforts' defined as at least three repeated attempts to serve at different times of day or on different days before declaring inability to serve
claims.source_title: Okla. Admin. Code § 340:25-5-190 - Service of process
claims.quote: Diligent efforts means at least three-repeated attempts to serve the individual at different times of day or on different days of the week, before declaring inability to serve.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(C)(5) provides that an acknowledgment on the back of the summons or voluntary appearance of a defendant is equivalent to service
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: 5. SERVICE BY ACKNOWLEDGMENT. An acknowledgment on the back of the summons or the voluntary appearance of a defendant is equivalent to service.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(C)(6) allows court-ordered alternative service methods when service cannot be made by personal delivery or mail, upon filing an affidavit of due diligence
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: 6. SERVICE BY OTHER METHODS. If service cannot be made by personal delivery or by mail, a defendant... may be served as provided by court order in a manner which is reasonably calculated to give the defendant actual notice of the proceedings and an opportunity to be heard and upon filing an affidavit by the plaintiff or plaintiff's attorney that with due diligence service cannot otherwise be made upon the defendant.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(C)(7) prohibits prisoners, parolees, or probationers from being appointed to serve process
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: 7. NO SERVICE BY PRISONER. No prisoner in any jail, Department of Corrections facility, private prison, or parolee or probationer under supervision of the Department of Corrections shall be appointed by any court to serve process on any defendant, party or witness.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(C)(4) designates the Secretary of State as agent for service of process on corporations that cannot be served by other methods
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: Service may also be made upon a domestic or foreign corporation by serving the Secretary of State as its agent... Within three (3) working days after receiving the summons and petition, the Secretary of State shall send notice by letter, certified mail, return receipt requested, directed to the corporation at its registered office

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(C)(3) provides for service by publication when due diligence cannot achieve service by other methods, requiring one day a week for three consecutive weeks in a newspaper authorized to publish legal notices
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: c. Service pursuant to this paragraph shall be made by publication of a notice, signed by the court clerk, one (1) day a week for three (3) consecutive weeks in a newspaper authorized by law to publish legal notices which is published in the county where the petition is filed.

claims.statement: 12 O.S. § 2004(H) allows courts to amend process or proof of service at any time unless material prejudice would result
claims.source_title: 12 OK Stat § 2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: H. AMENDMENT. At any time in its discretion and upon such terms as it deems just, the court may allow any process or proof of service thereof to be amended, unless it clearly appears that material prejudice would result to the substantial rights of the party against whom the process issued.

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(c)(2) states that any person who is at least 18 years old and not a party may serve a summons and complaint in federal court.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: Any person who is at least 18 years old and not a party may serve a summons and complaint.

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(c)(3) provides that at the plaintiff's request, the court may order service by a United States marshal, deputy marshal, or a person specially appointed by the court. The court must so order if the plaintiff is authorized to proceed in forma pauperis under 28 U.S.C. §1915 or as a seaman under 28 U.S.C. §1916.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: At the plaintiff's request, the court may order that service be made by a United States marshal or deputy marshal or by a person specially appointed by the court. The court must so order if the plaintiff is authorized to proceed in forma pauperis under 28 U.S.C. §1915 or as a seaman under 28 U.S.C. §1916.

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(e) provides that an individual within a judicial district of the United States may be served by: (1) delivering a copy of the summons and complaint to the individual personally; (2) leaving a copy at the individual's dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; or (3) delivering a copy to an agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: An individual within a judicial district of the United States may be served in the following ways: (1) by delivering a copy of the summons and of the complaint to the individual personally; (2) by leaving a copy of each at the individual's dwelling house or usual place of abode with some person of suitable age and discretion then residing therein; or (3) by delivering a copy of each to an agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process.

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(h) requires service on a domestic or foreign corporation, partnership, or unincorporated association by delivering a copy to an officer, managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process, or in the manner prescribed by Rule 4(e)(1) for serving an individual.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: Unless federal law provides otherwise or the defendant's waiver has been filed, a domestic or foreign corporation, or a partnership or other unincorporated association that is subject to suit under a common name, must be served: (1) in a judicial district of the United States: (A) in the manner prescribed by Rule 4(e)(1) for serving an individual; or (B) by delivering a copy of the summons and of the complaint to an officer, a managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(m) requires that service of a defendant be completed within 90 days after the complaint is filed. If not, the court must dismiss the action without prejudice or order service within a specified time, unless the plaintiff shows good cause for failure.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: If a defendant is not served within 90 days after the complaint is filed, the court—on motion or on its own after notice to the plaintiff—must dismiss the action without prejudice against that defendant or order that service be made within a specified time. But if the plaintiff shows good cause for the failure, the court must extend the time for service for an appropriate period.

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(l)(1) requires proof of service by the server's affidavit, except for service by a United States marshal or deputy marshal.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: Unless service is waived, proof of service must be made to the court. Except for service by a United States marshal or deputy marshal, proof must be by the server's affidavit.

claims.statement: FRCP Rule 4(d) allows a plaintiff to request that a defendant waive formal service of a summons. If the defendant fails to waive service without good cause, the court must impose on the defendant the expenses later incurred in making service.
claims.source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: An individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service under Rule 4(e), (f), or (h) has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons. The plaintiff may notify such a defendant that an action has been commenced and request that the defendant waive service of a summons.

claims.statement: Oklahoma adopted the Uniform Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act (UIDDA) on April 23, 2021, when Governor J. Kevin Stitt signed HB 2229 into law. The Act went into effect on November 1, 2021.
claims.source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
claims.quote: On April 23, 2021, Gov. J. Kevin Stitt signed HB 2229 into law. Commonly known as the Uniform Deposition and Discovery Act (UIDDA), this new provision of Oklahoma law went into effect Nov. 1, 2021, codified in 12 O.S. §3250, et seq., (OSCN 2022).

claims.statement: Oklahoma's UIDDA is codified at 12 O.S. §§ 3250 through 3257, under Chapter 41B - Uniform Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act of 2021.
claims.source_title: Title 12 of the Oklahoma Statutes - OSCN
claims.quote: Chapter 41B - Uniform Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act of 2021: § 3250. Short Title; § 3251. Definitions; § 3252. Subpoena - Procedure; § 3253. Subpoena - Service; § 3254. Subpoena - Response; § 3255. Application for Protective Order, or to Enforce, Quash or Modify Subpoena; § 3256. Uniformity of the Law Among the States; § 3257. Discovery Requests in Cases Pending on November 1, 2021

claims.statement: Under 12 O.S. § 3252, a party seeking to serve an out-of-state subpoena in Oklahoma must submit a foreign subpoena to a clerk of court in the Oklahoma county in which discovery is sought. The clerk shall promptly issue a subpoena for service upon the person to which the foreign subpoena is directed.
claims.source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
claims.quote: Section 12 O.S. §3252 sets forth the procedure whereby a foreign subpoena is honored in Oklahoma. Specifically, 'a party must submit a foreign subpoena to a clerk of court in the county in which discovery is sought' in Oklahoma. The UIDDA provides that the clerk, upon receipt of the foreign subpoena, 'in accordance with that court's procedure, shall promptly issue a subpoena for service upon the person to which the foreign subpoena is directed.'

claims.statement: The Oklahoma subpoena issued under UIDDA must incorporate the terms used in the foreign subpoena and contain or be accompanied by the names, addresses, and telephone numbers of all counsel of record and any party not represented by counsel. A request by a foreign attorney for issuance of an Oklahoma subpoena pursuant to the UIDDA does not constitute an appearance in the courts of Oklahoma.
claims.source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
claims.quote: Section 12 O.S. §3252(C)(1-2) provides the Oklahoma subpoena must 'incorporate the terms used in the foreign subpoena; and contain or be accompanied by the names, addresses and telephone numbers of all counsel of record in the proceeding with which the subpoena related and of any party not represented by counsel.' Notably, a request by a foreign attorney for issuance of an Oklahoma subpoena pursuant to the UIDDA 'does not constitute an appearance in the courts of [Oklahoma].'

claims.statement: Under 12 O.S. § 3253, a subpoena issued under the UIDDA must be served in accordance with 12 O.S. § 2004.1, and the duties of a person served are governed by 12 O.S. § 2004.1(D).
claims.source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
claims.quote: Once an Oklahoma subpoena is issued containing provisions consistent with those found in the foreign subpoena, 12 O.S. §3253 states the Oklahoma subpoena must be served in accordance with 12 O.S. §2004.1. With respect to the duties of a person in Oklahoma served with such a subpoena, the UIDDA again refers us to 12 O.S. §2004.1, this time expressly incorporating the language found in 12 O.S. §2004.1(D).

claims.statement: Under 12 O.S. § 3255, a party may apply for a protective order or to enforce, quash, or modify an issued subpoena. Such applications must be submitted in the county in which discovery is to be conducted and are governed by the rules or statutes of Oklahoma.
claims.source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
claims.quote: Section 3255 makes clear that litigation regarding the subpoena is governed by the 'rules or statutes of this state,' and requests to enforce, quash or modify the subpoena must be 'submitted in the county in which discovery is to be conducted.'

claims.statement: To serve an Oklahoma subpoena in another state, an Oklahoma attorney should locate the version of the UIDDA adopted in the target state, contact the court clerk in the county where the subpoena will be served, submit the Oklahoma subpoena with any required fee, and the clerk will issue a local subpoena conforming to the Oklahoma subpoena, which is then served pursuant to the foreign jurisdiction's procedures.
claims.source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
claims.quote: Typically, the court clerk will require a letter invoking the local version of the UIDDA and the payment of a small fee for issuance of the foreign subpoena. Once the clerk receives your duly issued Oklahoma subpoena, they should then issue a local subpoena conforming to the Oklahoma subpoena. The local subpoena will then be transmitted back to counsel for service pursuant to the foreign jurisdiction's procedures for service of subpoenas.

claims.statement: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) is codified at 50 U.S.C. §§ 3901-4043 and applies to all civil proceedings, including state court proceedings, to protect servicemembers on active duty.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: This section applies to any civil action or proceeding, including any child custody proceeding, in which the defendant does not make an appearance.

claims.statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(b)(1) requires that before a default judgment may be entered against a defendant who does not appear, the plaintiff must file an affidavit stating whether or not the defendant is in military service and showing necessary facts in support of the affidavit.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: (1) Affidavit requirement. If a defendant does not make an appearance in an action described in subsection (a), the court may not enter a default judgment against the defendant until the plaintiff files an affidavit-- (A) stating whether or not the defendant is in military service and showing necessary facts in support of the affidavit; or (B) if the plaintiff is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service, stating that the plaintiff is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service.

claims.statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(b)(2) provides that if a court determines that a defendant is in military service, the court may not enter a judgment until after the court appoints an attorney to represent the defendant.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: If the court determines that the defendant is in military service, the court may not enter a judgment until after the court appoints an attorney to represent the defendant.

claims.statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(b)(3) allows the court to require the plaintiff to post a bond as a condition of entry of a default judgment if the court is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service. The bond may be used to indemnify the defendant if later found to be a servicemember.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: If the court is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service, the court may-- (A) require the plaintiff to file a bond in an amount approved by the court to indemnify the defendant against any loss or damage the defendant may suffer by reason of any judgment entered against the defendant, should the defendant later prove to have been in military service at the time the judgment was entered; and (B) make such other disposition as may be equitable.

claims.statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(d) requires a court to grant a stay of proceedings for a minimum period of 90 days when a defendant in military service has not appeared, if the court determines there may be a defense that cannot be presented without the defendant's presence, or if appointed counsel cannot contact the defendant after due diligence.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: In an action covered by this section in which the defendant is in military service, the court shall grant a stay of proceedings for a minimum period of 90 days under this subsection upon application of counsel, or on the court's own motion, if the court determines that-- (1) there may be a defense to the action and a defense cannot be presented without the presence of the defendant; or (2) after due diligence, counsel has been unable to contact the defendant or otherwise determine if a meritorious defense exists.

claims.statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(g) allows a servicemember to have a default judgment entered during active duty (or within 60 days after termination) reopened upon application filed not later than 90 days after termination or release from military service, if the servicemember was prejudiced by military service in making a defense and has a meritorious defense.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: If a default judgment is entered in an action covered by this section against a servicemember during the servicemember's period of military service (or within 60 days after termination of or release from such military service), the court entering the judgment shall, upon application by or on behalf of the servicemember, reopen the judgment... if it appears that-- (A) the servicemember was materially affected by reason of that military service in making a defense to the action; and (B) the servicemember has a meritorious or legal defense to the action or some part of it.

claims.statement: The SCRA affidavit requirement may be satisfied by a statement, declaration, verification, or certificate in writing subscribed and certified or declared to be true under penalty of perjury. Knowingly filing a false affidavit carries criminal penalties of fine or imprisonment for not more than one year, or both.
claims.source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: The requirement for an affidavit under paragraph (1) may be satisfied by a statement, declaration, verification, or certificate, in writing, subscribed and certified or declared to be true under penalty of perjury. (c) A person who makes or uses an affidavit permitted under subsection (b) knowing it to be false, shall be fined as provided in title 18, or imprisoned for not more than one year, or both.

claims.statement: Military service status under the SCRA can be verified through the Defense Manpower Data Center (DMDC) website at https://scra.dmdc.osd.mil. A Single Record Request can be submitted to obtain a report certifying Title 10 active duty status.
claims.source_title: SCRA - Servicemembers Civil Relief Act | DMDC
claims.quote: Users may submit a Single Record Request to obtain a report certifying Title 10 active duty status for provisions under SCRA. An account is required to use Single Record Request.

claims.statement: The DMDC SCRA website requires the individual's last name and Social Security number for verification. If the Social Security number is not available, a manual search can be requested by mail to: Defense Manpower Data Center, Attn: Military Verification, 1600 Wilson Blvd., Suite 400, Arlington, VA 22209-2593.
claims.source_title: Plaintiff's Guide to SCRA Compliance - Maryland Courts
claims.quote: If the plaintiff has the last name and social security number of the defendant, the plaintiff may access the Defense Manpower Data Center web site at https://www.scra.dmdc.osd.mil The plaintiff must enter the defendant's last name and social security number. If a Social Security number is not available, verification may also be made by contacting the DOD at: Defense Manpower Data Center, Attn: Military Verification, 1600 Wilson Blvd. Suite 400, Arlington, Virginia 22209-2593.

claims.statement: The SCRA covers members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard on active duty; members of the Reserves on federal active duty; members of the National Guard called to active duty for more than 30 days under federal orders; and commissioned officers of the Public Health Service and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
claims.source_title: Servicemembers' Civil Relief Act (SCRA) | United States Courts
claims.quote: The Act applies to members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard on active duty as defined in 10 U.S.C. 101(d)(1). The Act may also apply to members of the National Guard, and commissioned members of the Public Health Service and the National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration.

claims.statement: The Military Lending Act (MLA) is a separate statute from the SCRA that protects active duty servicemembers and their dependents at the point of credit origination, capping interest and fees at 36% Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR). The MLA uses the same DMDC verification system as the SCRA.
claims.source_title: SCRA and MLA: What is the Difference? | Experian
claims.quote: The Servicemember Civil Relief Act (SCRA) protects service members and their dependents (indirectly) on existing debts when the service member becomes active duty. In contrast, the Military Lending Act (MLA) protects service members, their spouses and/or covered dependents at point of origination if they are on active duty at that time. The MLA limits interest rates and fees to 36 percent Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR).

claims.statement: The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) is codified at 15 U.S.C. §§ 1692-1692p and applies to the collection of debts incurred by consumers primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: This subchapter may be cited as the 'Fair Debt Collection Practices Act.' 15 U.S.C. 1692a: The term 'debt' means any obligation or alleged obligation of a consumer to pay money arising out of a transaction in which the money, property, insurance or services which are the subject of the transaction are primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(a)(1) prohibits debt collectors from communicating with consumers at unusual times, defined as before 8:00 a.m. and after 9:00 p.m. local time at the consumer's location, unless the consumer consents.
claims.source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: Without the prior consent of the consumer given directly to the debt collector or the express permission of a court of competent jurisdiction, a debt collector may not communicate with a consumer in connection with the collection of any debt-- (1) at any unusual time or place or a time or place known or which should be known to be inconvenient to the consumer. In the absence of knowledge of circumstances to the contrary, a debt collector shall assume that the convenient time for communicating with a consumer is after 8 o'clock antemeridian and before 9 o'clock postmeridian, local time at the consumer's location.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(a)(2) prohibits a debt collector from communicating with a consumer who is represented by an attorney, if the debt collector knows or can readily ascertain the attorney's name and address, unless the attorney fails to respond or consents to direct communication.
claims.source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: if the debt collector knows the consumer is represented by an attorney with respect to such debt and has knowledge of, or can readily ascertain, such attorney's name and address, unless the attorney fails to respond within a reasonable period of time to a communication from the debt collector or unless the attorney consents to direct communication with the consumer.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(b) prohibits debt collectors from communicating with third parties about a debt without the consumer's prior consent or court permission, except as reasonably necessary to effectuate a postjudgment judicial remedy or as permitted under section 1692b (location information).
claims.source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: Except as provided in section 1692b of this title, without the prior consent of the consumer given directly to the debt collector, or the express permission of a court of competent jurisdiction, or as reasonably necessary to effectuate a postjudgment judicial remedy, a debt collector may not communicate, in connection with the collection of any debt, with any person other than the consumer, his attorney, a consumer reporting agency if otherwise permitted by law, the creditor, the attorney of the creditor, or the attorney of the debt collector.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(c) allows a consumer to stop all communication from a debt collector by sending a written request. Upon receipt, the debt collector must cease further communication except to advise of termination of efforts, notify of remedies, or notify of intent to invoke a specified remedy.
claims.source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: If a consumer notifies a debt collector in writing that the consumer refuses to pay a debt or that the consumer wishes the debt collector to cease further communication with the consumer, the debt collector shall not communicate further with the consumer with respect to such debt, except-- (1) to advise the consumer that the debt collector's further efforts are being terminated; (2) to notify the consumer that the debt collector or creditor may invoke specified remedies which are ordinarily invoked by such debt collector or creditor; or (3) where applicable, to notify the consumer that the debt collector or creditor intends to invoke a specified remedy.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692d prohibits harassment or abuse in debt collection, including the use or threat of violence, obscene or profane language, publication of lists of consumers who refuse to pay debts, and repeatedly or continuously causing a telephone to ring with intent to annoy.
claims.source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692d - Harassment or abuse | LII / Cornell Law School
claims.quote: A debt collector may not engage in any conduct the natural consequence of which is to harass, oppress, or abuse any person in connection with the collection of a debt. Without limiting the general application of the foregoing, the following conduct is a violation of this section: (1) The use or threat of use of violence or other criminal means... (2) The use of obscene or profane language... (5) Causing a telephone to ring or engaging any person in telephone conversation repeatedly or continuously with intent to annoy, abuse, or harass any person at the called number.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692e prohibits false, deceptive, or misleading representations in connection with debt collection, including misrepresenting the amount of a debt, falsely implying legal action will be taken, using false representation of the legal status of any debt, and using any false representation or deceptive means to collect a debt.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
