claims.quote: A debt collector may not use any false, deceptive, or misleading representation or means in connection with the collection of any debt. Without limiting the general application of the foregoing, the following conduct is a violation of this section: (2)(A) The false representation of the character, amount, or legal status of any debt... (10) The use of any false representation or deceptive means to collect or attempt to collect any debt or to obtain information concerning a consumer.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692f prohibits unfair practices by debt collectors, including collecting amounts not expressly authorized, accepting postdated checks by more than five days without written notice, depositing postdated checks prior to the date shown, taking nonjudicial action to dispossess property without a present right, and communicating by postcard.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: A debt collector may not use unfair or unconscionable means to collect or attempt to collect any debt. Without limiting the general application of the foregoing, the following conduct is a violation of this section: (1) The collection of any amount (including any interest, fee, charge, or expense incidental to the principal obligation) unless such amount is expressly authorized by the agreement creating the debt or permitted by law. (4) Depositing or threatening to deposit any postdated check or other postdated payment instrument prior to the date on such check or instrument. (7) Communicating with a consumer regarding a debt by post card.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692g requires a debt collector to provide a written validation notice within five days of the initial communication, including: the amount of the debt; the name of the creditor; a statement that the consumer has 30 days to dispute the debt; and notice that upon written dispute, the collector will obtain verification or a copy of any judgment.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: Within five days after the initial communication with a consumer in connection with the collection of any debt, a debt collector shall, unless the following information is contained in the initial communication or the consumer has paid the debt, send the consumer a written notice containing-- (1) the amount of the debt; (2) the name of the creditor to whom the debt is owed; (3) a statement that unless the consumer, within thirty days after receipt of the notice, disputes the validity of the debt, or any portion thereof, the debt will be assumed to be valid by the debt collector; (4) a statement that if the consumer notifies the debt collector in writing within the thirty-day period that the debt, or any portion thereof, is disputed, the debt collector will obtain verification of the debt or a copy of a judgment against the consumer and a copy of such verification or judgment will be mailed to the consumer by the debt collector.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692g(b) provides that if the consumer disputes the debt in writing within 30 days, the debt collector must cease collection efforts until verification or a copy of the judgment is mailed to the consumer.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: If the consumer notifies the debt collector in writing within the thirty-day period described in subsection (a) that the debt, or any portion thereof, is disputed, or that the consumer requests the name and address of the original creditor, the debt collector shall cease collection of the debt, or any disputed portion thereof, until the debt collector obtains verification of the debt or a copy of a judgment, or the name and address of the original creditor, and a copy of such verification or judgment, or name and address of the original creditor, is mailed to the consumer by the debt collector.

claims.statement: The FDCPA defines 'debt collector' to include any person who regularly collects debts owed to another, but excludes officers or employees of a creditor collecting debts in the creditor's name, and creditors collecting their own debts under their own name.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: The term 'debt collector' means any person who uses any instrumentality of interstate commerce or the mails in any business the principal purpose of which is the collection of any debts, or who regularly collects or attempts to collect, directly or indirectly, debts owed or due or asserted to be owed or due another. The term does not include-- (A) any officer or employee of a creditor while, in the name of the creditor, collecting debts for such creditor.

claims.statement: CFPB Regulation F (12 CFR Part 1006), effective November 30, 2021, implements the FDCPA and provides additional requirements including: a presumption of compliance for call frequency (no more than 7 calls within 7 days to a particular consumer); expanded requirements for electronic communications; and a model validation notice form with safe harbor protections.
claims.source_title: Bona Fide Error Defense for Final Debt Collection Rule | Smith Debnam
claims.quote: In 2020 and 2021, the CFPB amended Regulation F. The amendments, which address a broader range of topics, such as debt collection communications, and restate the substantive provisions of the FDCPA, became effective on November 30, 2021. Section 1006.14 establishes a bright line by placing numeric limitations on the placing of telephone calls... Section 1006.34 of the Rule reinvents the Debt Validation Notice by requiring significantly more robust disclosures.

claims.statement: Oklahoma does not have state-specific debt collection laws that regulate third-party debt collectors beyond the federal FDCPA. Oklahoma residents are protected solely by the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. However, the Oklahoma Consumer Protection Act (Okla. Stat. tit. 15, §§ 751-763) provides broader consumer protections.
claims.source_title: Debt Collection Laws in Oklahoma: Know Your Rights | Upsolve
claims.quote: Oklahoma doesn't have any state-specific laws that regulate third-party debt collectors or original creditors. But residents are still fully protected by the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA).

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692k provides for civil liability for FDCPA violations, including actual damages, additional statutory damages up to $1,000 per violation, attorney's fees, and costs. In class actions, statutory damages are capped at the lesser of $500,000 or 1% of the debt collector's net worth.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: Any debt collector who fails to comply with any provision of this subchapter with respect to any person is liable to such person in an amount equal to the sum of-- (1) any actual damage sustained by such person as a result of such failure; (2)(A) in the case of any action by an individual, such additional damages as the court may allow, but not exceeding $1,000; or (B) in the case of a class action, such amount for each named plaintiff as could be recovered under subparagraph (A), and such amount as the court may allow for all other class members, without regard to a minimum individual recovery, not to exceed the lesser of $500,000 or 1 per centum of the net worth of the debt collector.

claims.statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692n provides that the FDCPA does not annul, alter, or affect any state law except to the extent that such state law is inconsistent with the FDCPA, and then only to the extent of the inconsistency.
claims.source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
claims.quote: This subchapter does not annul, alter, or affect, or exempt any person subject to the provisions of this subchapter from complying with the laws of any State with respect to debt collection practices, except to the extent that those laws are inconsistent with any provision of this subchapter, and then only to the extent of the inconsistency.

claims.statement: The Cherokee Nation District Court serves all 14 counties of the Cherokee Nation Reservation and handles civil, criminal, and juvenile cases. The court is located at 17675 S. Muskogee Ave, 2nd Floor, Tahlequah, OK 74464.
claims.source_title: Cherokee Nation District Court - Official Page
claims.quote: The District Court of the Cherokee Nation serves all 14 counties of the Cherokee Nation Reservation. The court handles civil, criminal, and juvenile cases. The civil realm can hear matters of name changes, protective orders, and juvenile guardianship. The criminal cases are those that arise from the reservation and are prosecuted by the Cherokee Nation Attorney General's Office.

claims.statement: Cherokee Nation Court Clerk's Office can be reached at (918) 207-3900 for general court information.
claims.source_title: Cherokee Nation Courts - Official Website
claims.quote: If you have any questions or concerns, please contact the Court Clerk's Office at 918-207-3900.

claims.statement: The Cherokee Nation Marshal Service provides one dedicated Marshal that serves as a court bailiff and process server on a regular basis.
claims.source_title: Cherokee Nation Supreme Court and District Court Report 2020
claims.quote: The Marshal service provides one dedicated Marshal that serves as a court bailiff and process server on a regular basis, but additional...

claims.statement: Cherokee Nation Supreme Court contact is cnsupremecourt@cherokee.org, phone (918) 207-3900, Court Clerk: P.O. Box 1097 Tahlequah, OK 74465.
claims.source_title: Cherokee Nation Supreme Court - Official Page
claims.quote: For more information on the Supreme Court, please call (918) 207-3900 or email at cnsupremecourt@cherokee.org. Court Clerk: P.O. Box 1097 Tahlequah, Ok 74465.

claims.source_title: Cherokee Nation Courts - Official Website
claims.quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

claims.statement: The Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure Section 51 defines process as any document used by the court to acquire or exercise jurisdiction over a person or specific property. All process shall be under the seal of the court clerk, signed by the court clerk, and dated.
claims.source_title: Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma Code of Civil Procedure
claims.quote: The style of all Process shall be 'The Choctaw Nation to:' and all process shall be under the seal of the court clerk and shall be signed by the court clerk, and dated the day it is issued.

claims.statement: The Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure Section 52 allows the court or judge to appoint a substitute for service of process. A person appointed has the same power as tribal police.
claims.source_title: Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma Code of Civil Procedure - Section 52
claims.quote: The court or judge thereof, or the clerk in the absence of the judge upon his oral or written order, for good cause, may appoint a person to serve a particular process or order, who shall have the same power to execute it which the tribal police have.

claims.statement: Tribal police or process server must endorse upon every summons the day and hour it was received (Section 53). Penalties for failure to execute or return process may be up to $1,000 (Section 54).
claims.source_title: Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure - Sections 53-54
claims.quote: The tribal police officer or the process server shall endorse upon every summons, order of arrest... the day and hour it was received by him... if he fail to do so... he shall be amerced by the court in a sum not exceeding One Thousand Dollars($1,000.00)

claims.statement: Choctaw Nation District Court has locations in Durant (2250 Chukka Hina, 580-920-7027), Talihina (302 Church Street, 918-567-3582), and McAlester (1133 S. George Nigh Expressway, 918-426-7654).
claims.source_title: Contact Choctaw Nation Judicial Branch
claims.quote: Choctaw Nation Tribal Courthouse. 302 Church Street. Talihina, OK 74571. 918-567-3582. Choctaw Nation Judicial Center. 2250 Chukka Hina. Durant, OK 74701. 580-920-7027. Choctaw Nation Judicial Center. 1133 S. George Nigh Expressway. McAlester, OK 74501. 918-426-7654.

claims.statement: Choctaw Nation Judicial Branch provides e-filing portal for electronic filing of court documents.
claims.source_title: Choctaw Nation E-Filing Portal
claims.quote: Get 24/7 access to electronically filed documents, receive proof of delivery, and instantly download file-stamped copies of your filings.

claims.statement: Choctaw Nation service of process may be performed by tribal law enforcement officers, persons licensed to serve process by any state or federal government, or persons specially appointed by the court.
claims.source_title: Choctaw Nation Code of Civil Procedure
claims.quote: Process, summons, subpoenas and service... A tribal law enforcement officer... A person licensed to serve process in civil cases by the Choctaw Nation, any state, or the federal government... A person specially appointed by the court for that purpose.

claims.statement: The Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department is located at 821 N. Mississippi, Ada, Oklahoma 74820. The District Court phone number is (580) 235-0279 and the Supreme Court phone number is (580) 235-0281.
claims.source_title: Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department - Official Website
claims.quote: Judicial Building. 821 N. Mississippi. Ada, Oklahoma 74820. (580) 235-0281. District Court. P.O. Box 129. Ada, Oklahoma 74821. (580) 235-0279.

claims.statement: The Chickasaw Nation District Court has jurisdiction over all civil actions arising under the Constitution, Laws or Treaties of the Chickasaw Nation. The District Court focuses on family issues (divorce, child custody, child support, adoption), civil matters, juvenile matters, guardianship, and criminal misdemeanors for crimes committed by Indians on tribal land.
claims.source_title: Chickasaw Nation District Court
claims.quote: The District Court has jurisdiction over all civil actions arising under the Constitution, Laws or Treaties of the Chickasaw Nation which arise in the tribal jurisdiction... The District Court will focus on family issues such as divorce, child custody, child support, parental rights, visitation and adoption, civil matters, juvenile matters, and guardianship.

claims.statement: Under Chickasaw Nation code, defendants must serve an answer within 20 days after service of summons and petition. Defenses including insufficiency of process and insufficiency of service of process may be asserted by motion.
claims.source_title: Chickasaw Nation Code - Title 06
claims.quote: A defendant shall serve his answer within twenty (20) days after the service of summons and petition upon him... defenses may at the option of the pleader be made by motion: d. insufficiency of process; e. insufficiency of service of process;

claims.statement: Service procedures not publicly available on Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department website for general process serving. Contact the District Court at (580) 235-0279 for specific service requirements.
claims.source_title: Chickasaw Nation Judicial Department
claims.quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

claims.statement: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court is located at the Mound Building, 2501 Lvmhvlke (Eagle) Street, Okmulgee, OK 74447. General questions: (918) 758-1400 or assistance@mcndistrictcourt.com.
claims.source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court - Official Website
claims.quote: Monday – Friday | 8:00 am – 5:00 pm. Muscogee (Creek) Nation Complex Mound Building P.O. Box 652 | Okmulgee, OK 74447. Phone: 918.758.1400

claims.statement: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court accepts only money orders or cashier's checks at the window - no cash payments.
claims.source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court
claims.quote: The District Court does not accept payment in Cash, only Money Orders or Cashier's checks are accepted at the window.

claims.statement: The Official Court Rules for the Muscogee (Creek) Nation Court are available on the District Court website.
claims.source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court Rules
claims.quote: The Official Court Rules for the Muscogee (Creek) Nation Court can be found here: Rules-of-the-MCN-District-Court

claims.statement: Service procedures not publicly available on Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court website for general process serving. Contact the court at assistance@mcndistrictcourt.com or (918) 758-1400 for specific service requirements.
claims.source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation District Court
claims.quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

claims.quote: SEMINOLE NATION TRIBAL COURT. P.O BOX 1220. 206 E. 2nd St. Wewoka, Oklahoma 74884. Main Number: (405) 257-7200

claims.source_title: Seminole Nation of Oklahoma
claims.quote: Service procedures not publicly available — contact tribal court clerk directly.

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation Court is located at Ki-Ho-Ta Center, 5681 S 630 Rd, Miami, OK 74354. Phone: 918-238-3173. Court Administrator: Corissa Millard. Court Clerk: Julio De Los Santos. Hours: Monday-Thursday 8 AM - 5 PM, Friday 8 AM - 4 PM.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Court Staff Directory
claims.quote: Physical Address: Ki-Ho-Ta Center, 5681 S 630 Rd, Miami, OK 74354. Phone: +1 9182383173. Hours: Monday through Thursday 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Friday 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. Staff: Millard, Corissa; De Los Santos, Julio

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation requires a $35 fee for initial process server license application, with $15 yearly renewal. Applicants must be at least 18, not convicted of a felony or crime of moral turpitude. Process servers must return a Return of Service form to the Court Clerk on the next business day after service.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Process Server Application
claims.quote: A fee of $35.00 will be collected with the initial application, a Quapaw Nation Process Server License number will be assigned and ID card. Licenses are renewable yearly, with a $15.00 fee... A Return of Service form provided by this Court will be filled out and returned by the Process Server to the Court Clerk, on the next business day after service.

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation has a licensing requirement for private process servers under Resolution 041809-A. Court may waive background investigation if applicant presents a current process server license from any Indian tribe or current state process server license.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Process Server Application - Resolution No. 041809-A
claims.quote: All applicants are subject to background investigation to verify the requirements for licensing, however the Court may waive this if the applicant presents current process servers license from any Indian tribe or current state process server license.

claims.statement: Quapaw Nation court rules require that service of process on an employee of the Quapaw Tribe must be made by contacting the Human Resource Department and setting a time of service with the employee in the Human Resource office.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Process Server Application - Service Rules
claims.quote: Service of process required on an employee of the Quapaw Tribe of Oklahoma or any of its entities, will be made by contacting the Human Resource Department and setting a time of service with the employee in the Human Resource office, so as not to interfere in the operations of said business.

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation has adopted Court Rules under Resolution 052023-H governing court operations, including procedures for civil procedure and service of process.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Court Codes
claims.quote: Resolution 052023-H: Adopting Court Rules - Formal adoption of procedural rules governing court operations.

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation court rules provide for dismissal if summons is not issued within 6 months or if issued and not served within 6 months.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Court Rules
claims.quote: If an action be commenced, but no summons issued within six (6) months, or if issued and not served within six (6) months, the Court may dismiss such action on its own motion and without notice.

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation Bar Association requires a certificate of good standing dated within 90 days. Pro Hac Vice fee is $25.00 per case filing. Annual membership fee is $40.00.
claims.source_title: Quapaw Nation Bar Association
claims.quote: An official, original certificate of good standing issued by the bar of the highest court of any state of the United States... dated no more the 90 calendar days prior... Pro Hac Vice fee is $25.00 per case filing... annual membership fee of $40.00 is due by January 31st.

claims.statement: McGirt v. Oklahoma, 591 U.S. ___ (2020), held that the Muscogee (Creek) Nation's reservation was never disestablished by Congress. The reservation remains 'Indian country' for purposes of criminal jurisdiction.
claims.source_title: McGirt v. Oklahoma, 591 U.S. (2020)
claims.quote: The Court held that Congress had never properly disestablished the Creek Nation's reservation in eastern Oklahoma. As a result, the Court concluded that the Creek Reservation remained 'Indian country.'

claims.statement: Following McGirt, the Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals extended the reasoning to recognize that the Cherokee Nation, Chickasaw Nation, Choctaw Nation, and Seminole Nation had pre-statehood reservations that Congress had never diminished or disestablished.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Oklahoma Cities and Towns in Indian Country
claims.quote: The Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals extended the reasoning in McGirt and held that the Cherokee Nation, Chickasaw Nation, Choctaw Nation and Seminole Nation had pre-statehood reservations that Congress had never diminished or disestablished.

claims.statement: Under McGirt and its progeny, the state of Oklahoma lacks criminal jurisdiction over all crimes committed by or against Indians on the reservations of the Five Tribes. The state continues to exercise jurisdiction over offenses by non-Indians against non-Indians in Indian country.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - McGirt Impact on Criminal Jurisdiction
claims.quote: The state of Oklahoma lacks criminal jurisdiction over all crimes committed by or against Indians on the reservations of the Five Tribes. The state continues to exercise jurisdiction over offenses by non-Indians against non-Indians in Indian country.

claims.statement: Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta, 597 U.S. (2022), held that the Federal Government and the State have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta, 597 U.S. (2022)
claims.quote: We conclude that the Federal Government and the State have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country.

claims.statement: After McGirt, the Five Tribes combined have filed in excess of 10,000 criminal cases. The Cherokee Nation went from filing less than 100 criminal cases a year pre-McGirt to almost 3,000 cases in nine months following the Hogner reservation decision.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - McGirt Impact on Tribal Courts
claims.quote: The Five Tribes combined have filed in excess of 10,000 criminal cases. The Cherokee Nation went from filing less than 100 criminal cases a year pre-McGirt, to almost 3,000 cases in the nine months following its reservation decision.

claims.statement: Following McGirt, tribal police forces, prosecutors' offices, courts, and juvenile justice programs grew at an exponential pace across all Five Tribes.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Tribal Court Expansion
claims.quote: Tribal police forces, prosecutors' offices, courts and juvenile justice programs have grown at an exponential pace. The Cherokee Nation Office of the Attorney General has gone from one full-time prosecutor to eight.

claims.statement: McGirt and its progeny recognized the Quapaw Nation reservation was never disestablished in State v. Lawhorn, 2021 OK CR 37.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Quapaw Nation Reservation
claims.quote: The OCCA has recently extended the McGirt analysis to the Quapaw Nation, finding the tribe's reservation was never disestablished in State v. Lawhorn, 2021 OK CR 37.

claims.statement: For service of process on tribal land, process servers must: (1) Research the specific tribal law, (2) Contact the Tribal Court Clerk and/or Tribal Police before attempting service, (3) File documents with the Tribal Court. Permission is often required to enter tribal lands for official business.
claims.source_title: Serving Papers on Tribal Land | Oklahoma Guide
claims.quote: Unlike in the state system, a process server generally does not have pre-granted authority to enter tribal lands to conduct official business. Permission is often required... Call the Tribal Court Clerk: This is the most direct way to get accurate, up-to-date information.

claims.statement: Under 12 O.S. Ch. 2 App., Rule 30, a tribal judgment may be filed with any Oklahoma district court clerk and enforced in the same manner as a state district court judgment. An affidavit with names and addresses must be filed, and no enforcement action may issue for 20 days.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Statutes - District Courts Rule 30 - Filing of Tribal Judgments
claims.quote: A copy of any tribal judgment authenticated in accordance with the applicable act of Congress or of the statutes of this state or the law of the tribe may be filed in the office of the district court clerk of any county of this state. The district court clerk shall treat the tribal judgment in the same manner as a judgment of the district court of any county of this state which may be enforced or satisfied in like manner.

claims.statement: Oklahoma law enforcement cross-deputization agreements and other tribal-state agreements are maintained on the Oklahoma Secretary of State website at www.sos.ok.gov/gov/tribal.aspx.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Tribal Cross-Deputization Agreements
claims.quote: The law enforcement cross-deputization agreements (and other tribal-state agreements and compacts) are all available on the Oklahoma Secretary of State website: www.sos.ok.gov/gov/tribal.aspx.

claims.statement: Cross-deputization agreements allow officers from one sovereign (tribal, state, local, or federal) to exercise the other's laws within specified boundaries. The legal basis includes the Indian Law Enforcement Reform Act (25 U.S.C. § 2801 et seq.) and the Oklahoma Interlocal Cooperation Act (74 O.S. § 1221 et seq.).
claims.source_title: Cross-Deputization in Oklahoma Tribal Law | Addison Law Firm
claims.quote: Cross-deputization agreements allow tribal and state/local officers to enforce each other's laws... The Indian Law Enforcement Reform Act (25 U.S.C. § 2801 et seq.) authorizes the Bureau of Indian Affairs to enter cooperative agreements with tribal and state agencies.

claims.statement: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation maintains an Intergovernmental Cross-Deputization Agreement with the United States and political subdivisions of the State of Oklahoma, authorized by NCA 92-15 § 108 and 74 O.S. §§ 1221 et seq.
claims.source_title: Muscogee (Creek) Nation Intergovernmental Cross-Deputization Agreement
claims.quote: The Muscogee (Creek) Nation has enacted legislation authorizing the Nation to enter into cross-deputization agreements with federal, state and tribal governments pursuant to NCA 92-15 § 108; and the Legislature of the State of Oklahoma has authorized the State and its political subdivisions to enter into cooperative agreements in accordance with the Oklahoma Inter-local Cooperation Act, 74 O.S. §§ 1221 et seq.

claims.statement: Historical cross-deputization agreement between Bureau of Indian Affairs, Muscogee (Creek) Nation, and Hughes County, Oklahoma was filed with Oklahoma Secretary of State in 1996.
claims.source_title: Cross-Deputization Agreement among Hughes County, BIA, and Muscogee (Creek) Nation
claims.quote: Enclosed please find a Cross-Deputization Agreement between the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA), the Muscogee (Creek) Nation of Oklahoma, and Hughes County, Oklahoma... filed with the Secretary of State

claims.statement: The Oklahoma History Society maintains a Tribal Compacts database listing cross-deputization agreements, intergovernmental cooperative agreements, and other tribal-state compacts dating back to 1991.
claims.source_title: Tribal Compacts - Oklahoma Historical Society
claims.quote: Cross-Deputization Agreement - Bureau of Indian Affairs / Ottawa County - May 21, 1992... Deputation Agreement - Chickasaw / State of Oklahoma - January 23, 2006... Tribal Addendum to Deputation Agreement - Quapaw / State of Oklahoma - May 19, 2011

claims.statement: Post-McGirt, the Cherokee Nation marshal is not aware of a single city or town wholly or partially within the Cherokee Nation Reservation that does not have a cross-deputization agreement with the nation.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Bar Association - Cross-Deputization Post-McGirt
claims.quote: The Cherokee Nation marshal is not aware of a single city or town that is wholly or partially within the Cherokee Nation Reservation that does not have a cross-deputization agreement with the nation.

claims.statement: The Quapaw Nation has a cross-deputization agreement effective March 1, 2022, and law enforcement agreements with Oklahoma Highway Patrol effective January 28, 2022.
claims.source_title: Serving Papers on Tribal Land - Quapaw Nation Cross-Deputization
claims.quote: Cross-deputization agreement (effective 3/1/2022), law enforcement agreements with Oklahoma Highway Patrol (1/28/2022)

claims.statement: Following Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta (2022), the State of Oklahoma and the federal government have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country. However, tribes lack criminal jurisdiction to prosecute non-Indians under Oliphant v. Suquamish Tribe unless the crime falls under VAWA.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta, 597 U.S. (2022)
claims.quote: We conclude that the Federal Government and the State have concurrent jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians against Indians in Indian country... Indian tribes lack criminal jurisdiction to prosecute crimes committed by non-Indians such as Castro-Huerta, even when non-Indians commit crimes against Indians in Indian country. See Oliphant v. Suquamish Tribe, 435 U.S. 191, 195 (1978).

claims.statement: Concurrent jurisdiction can create a 'pass the buck' dynamic where federal authorities reduce involvement when state authorities are present, potentially resulting in fewer prosecutions and less public safety in Indian country.
claims.source_title: Passing the Buck: The Perils of Oklahoma v. Castro-Huerta
claims.quote: Concurrent jurisdiction increases the number of law enforcement agents with jurisdiction, it also creates opportunities for those law enforcement agents to shirk responsibility... concurrent jurisdiction has sometimes 'created a pass-the-buck dynamic... with the end result being fewer police and more crime.'

claims.statement: McGirt v. Oklahoma recognized that reservations of the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Muscogee Creek, and Seminole) remain 'Indian country' as defined by 18 U.S.C. § 1151, covering approximately 19 million acres in eastern Oklahoma.
claims.source_title: McGirt v. Oklahoma - Chief Justice Roberts Dissent
claims.quote: Not only does the Court discover a Creek reservation that spans three million acres and includes most of the city of Tulsa, but the Court's reasoning portends that there are four more such reservations in Oklahoma. The rediscovered reservations encompass the entire eastern half of the State—19 million acres.

claims.statement: Oklahoma requires private process servers to be licensed under 12 O.S. § 158.1. Any person who knowingly and willfully serves process without a license is guilty of a misdemeanor.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: Any person who knowingly and willfully serves process in Oklahoma without a process server license issued by the State of Oklahoma or who holds himself or herself out to be a process server licensed by the State of Oklahoma when the person is not licensed as such shall, upon conviction, be guilty of a misdemeanor.

claims.statement: To obtain an Oklahoma process server license, applicant must be 18+, of good moral character, ethically and mentally fit, Oklahoma resident for 6+ months, and county resident for 30+ days.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: Any person who is: 1. Eighteen (18) years of age or older; 2. Of good moral character; 3. Found ethically and mentally fit; 4. A resident of the State of Oklahoma for a period of not less than six (6) months; and 5. A resident of the county or judicial administrative district in which the application is submitted for a period of not less than thirty (30) days, may obtain a license by filing an application with the court clerk on a verified form.

claims.statement: Persons convicted of a violent crime (as defined in 57 O.S. § 571) or any crime requiring sex offender registration are prohibited from obtaining or renewing a process server license in Oklahoma.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: Any person who has been convicted of a violent crime, as defined in Section 571 of Title 57 of the Oklahoma Statutes, or a crime that requires the person to register pursuant to the Sex Offenders Registration Act in this state or another state shall be prohibited from applying for or obtaining a license to serve process in this state.

claims.statement: The statewide process server license fee is $150.00 plus regular docketing, posting, mailing, and filing fees. Initial license is valid for 1 year, then renewable for 3-year periods at $15 per renewal.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: The applicant filing for a license to serve process anywhere in this state shall pay a license fee of One Hundred Fifty Dollars ($150.00), and the regular docketing, posting, mailing, and filing fees prescribed by law... At the end of one (1) calendar year from the date of issuance of the initial license, the license shall be renewed for a period of three (3) years... A fee of Fifteen Dollars ($15.00) per renewal shall be charged for each statewide license renewal.

claims.statement: A $5,000 surety bond running to the State of Oklahoma is required for faithful performance of duties before a process server license is granted.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: upon executing bond running to the State of Oklahoma in the amount of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000.00) for faithful performance of his or her duties and filing the bond with the court clerk, the applicant shall be authorized and licensed to serve court documents in any county in this state.

claims.statement: Tulsa County charges $364.14 for a new statewide process server license (including all fees), $229.14 for renewals, and requires a $35 publication fee within 10 days of application.
claims.source_title: Tulsa County Court Clerk - Family Department (Private Process Server Licensing)
claims.quote: New license filing fee is $364.14; Renewals are $229.14; You will also be required to publish a notice within 10 days of application. There is a $35.00 fee.

claims.statement: Tulsa County requires new process server applicants to: secure a $5,000 bond, wait a 30-day posting period, provide two 2x2 photographs. First-time applicants receive a 1-year license; successive renewals are for 3 years.
claims.source_title: Tulsa County Court Clerk - Family Department (Private Process Server Licensing)
claims.quote: In order to receive a license as a new process server an individual must: Secure a $5,000 bond for the new license; Wait a 30 day posting period; A first time applicant will be issued a 1 year license; Every successive renewal thereafter will be issued for 3 years; Applicants must bring two (2) copies of a 2'x2' photograph.

claims.statement: Oklahoma County (and other counties) follow the same statutory framework under 12 O.S. § 158.1 with statewide license. McCurtain County charges $364.14 new / $229.14 renewal for statewide license.
claims.source_title: McCurtain County - Private Process Server Licensing
claims.quote: New Process Server - Statewide $364.14 - Good for one (1) year / Process is the same as a new license/Statewide; Renewal Process Server - Statewide $229.14 - Good for three (3) years / Process is the same as a new license/Statewide

claims.statement: Oklahoma service of process is governed by 12 O.S. § 2004. Personal delivery is the primary method; substituted service may be made by leaving copies at the dwelling with a person 15+ years old residing therein.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Statutes §12-2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: upon an individual other than an infant who is less than fifteen (15) years of age or an incompetent person, by delivering a copy of the summons and of the petition personally or by leaving copies thereof at the person's dwelling house or usual place of abode with some person then residing therein who is fifteen (15) years of age or older

claims.statement: Service of summons must be made within 180 days after filing of the petition. If not served within 180 days without good cause, the action is deemed dismissed without prejudice.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Statutes §12-2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: If service of process is not made upon a defendant within one hundred eighty (180) days after the filing of the petition and the plaintiff has not shown good cause why such service was not made within that period, the action shall be deemed dismissed as to that defendant without prejudice.

claims.statement: Proof of service must be shown by affidavit and must set forth the county of issuance, name of person served, date, place, and method of service.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Statutes §12-2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: If service is made by a person other than a sheriff or deputy sheriff, the licensed process server shall make affidavit thereof. The return shall set forth the county of issuance, the name of the person served and the date, place and method of service.

claims.statement: No prisoner in any jail, Department of Corrections facility, private prison, or parolee or probationer under supervision of the Department of Corrections shall be appointed by any court to serve process.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Statutes §12-2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: No prisoner in any jail, Department of Corrections facility, private prison, or parolee or probationer under supervision of the Department of Corrections shall be appointed by any court to serve process on any defendant, party or witness.

claims.statement: In Oklahoma, there are no legal restrictions on the days or hours when process servers can serve legal documents; they are authorized to serve documents 24/7. Industry best practice recommends reasonable hours (approximately 6 AM to 10 PM).
claims.source_title: Understanding Process Service Regulations and Restrictions in Oklahoma
claims.quote: In Oklahoma, unlike some states, there are no legal restrictions on the days or hours when process servers can serve legal documents. They are authorized to serve documents 24/7. However, it's important to note that process servers typically attempt service during reasonable hours, around 6 AM to 10 PM, to maximize their chances of finding the recipient and to avoid potential safety concerns.

claims.statement: Process servers in Oklahoma cannot: trespass on private property without permission, impersonate law enforcement, harass or intimidate recipients, serve warrants or arrest documents, or break into homes or businesses.
claims.source_title: Understanding Process Service Regulations and Restrictions in Oklahoma
claims.quote: Geographic restrictions: County-licensed process servers can only serve process within their authorized county. Statewide licenses allow service throughout Oklahoma. Service limitations: Process servers cannot serve warrants, arrest warrants, or other documents that authorize taking property or arresting someone. Conduct restrictions: Process servers cannot harass, intimidate, or impersonate law enforcement while serving process.

claims.statement: A process server in Oklahoma cannot force their way onto private property without the owner's consent. If consent is denied, the server must leave and find another method of service.
claims.source_title: Learn How Far Can a Process Server Go in Piedmont, Okc?
claims.quote: No, a process server in Oklahoma City cannot force its way onto private property without the owner's consent. If the legal agent in OKC does not have consent and they try to enter private property, they could be charged with trespassing.

claims.statement: NAPPS (National Association of Professional Process Servers) Code of Ethics requires members to: perform all work lawfully and professionally, keep current all licenses/bonds/permits, promptly return proofs of service, promptly pay for services rendered by other members, and maintain confidentiality of clients.
claims.source_title: NAPPS Membership Code Of Ethics
claims.quote: All work shall be performed in a lawful, professional and ethical manner... Each member agrees to comply with and keep current during the tenure of his membership all necessary business licenses, bonds, permits and any other requirements mandated by all jurisdictions in which the member conducts business... All proofs of service shall be returned promptly upon completing the work order... Each member agrees to promptly pay for services rendered by another member unless other specific arrangements have been made.

claims.statement: NAPPS Best Practices state that service of process should be performed by a disinterested third party; in-person delivery to the named party is the preferred method; a written permanent record of service should be maintained and made available upon request.
claims.source_title: NAPPS Process Server Best Practices And Standards
claims.quote: Service of process or other papers for the purpose of acquiring jurisdiction over a person or property should be performed by a disinterested third party. The preferred and most effective method of service of process upon a party is in-person delivery of process to the named party... A written permanent record of the service should be maintained, and made available upon request.

claims.statement: NAPPS Best Practices require that a proof or affidavit of service must accurately state the date, time, place, and manner of service. It is not proper for a proof to be signed before completion or signed in blank.
claims.source_title: NAPPS Process Server Best Practices And Standards
claims.quote: A proof or affidavit of service must accurately state the date, time, place, and manner of service, and any additional information that would reflect how delivery of process or other legal document was made to a person or entity served... It is not proper for a proof of service to be signed before completion, or signed in blank to be completed later. It is not permissible to sign the process server's name to a proof of service on his or her behalf.

claims.statement: NAPPS membership requirements: no felony convictions (unless expunged/pardoned), affiliated with process serving for minimum 1 year, must abide by NAPPS Bylaws and Code of Ethics, provide valid driver's license, $175 annual dues plus $25 application fee.
claims.source_title: How To Become A Process Serving Member of NAPPS
claims.quote: No felony convictions that have not been expunged or pardoned. Affiliated with the process serving profession for a minimum of one year. Must abide by the NAPPS Bylaws and Code of Ethics. Provide a copy of a valid driver's license. Pay $175.00 annual dues and $25.00 application fee.

claims.statement: Joseph Iannazzi is a NAPPS member with Member ID 14801, joined in 2025. He operates Just Legal Solutions in Glenpool, Oklahoma and serves Tulsa, Wagoner, Rogers, and Osage counties primarily.
claims.source_title: National Association of Professional Process Servers - Member Profile
claims.quote: Name: Iannazzi, Joseph; Member Since: 2025; Mailing: Just Legal Solutions 564 E 138th Pl Glenpool, Oklahoma 74033; Services: PS CF CRS PC ST (Fee in advance) E-Filing, Courier; Counties Served: Tulsa, Wagoner, Rogers, Osage

claims.statement: Joseph Iannazzi is a licensed Oklahoma process server (12 O.S. § 158.1), commissioned Notary Public, certified Remote Online Notary, NAPPS Member ID 14801, Snapdocs-verified, and founder of Just Legal Solutions.
claims.source_title: Joseph Iannazzi | Founder & CEO, Just Legal Solutions
claims.quote: Licensed and bonded Oklahoma process server, commissioned Notary Public and Remote Online Notary, NAPPS Member ID 14801, Snapdocs-verified, Northeastern State University alumnus, and founder of Oklahoma's most transparent process serving and mobile notary firm.

claims.statement: NAPPS Position on Photography/Video: Due to serious privacy and safety concerns, process servers should not be required to photograph or videotape the subjects they are serving. Recording individuals while being served can violate their privacy rights.
claims.source_title: NAPPS Process Server Best Practices And Standards - Position on Photography or Video as Proof of Service
claims.quote: In response to this trend, the National Association of Professional Process Servers has sought the advice of counsel and now takes the position that, in light of serious privacy and safety concerns, process servers should not be required to photograph or videotape the subjects they are being served. A legal assessment of this practice demonstrates that recording individuals while they are being served can violate their privacy rights.

claims.statement: GPS tracking is widely recommended as a best practice in the process serving industry for documenting the exact location of service attempts, providing irrefutable evidence of compliance.
claims.source_title: Best Practices for Documenting Attempts to Serve Legal Papers
claims.quote: GPS Tracking: Recording the exact location of service attempts provides irrefutable evidence of compliance. Digital Cameras: Photos of the property or attempted service location add visual context to documentation.

claims.statement: Professional liability insurance (E&O) is not statutorily required for Oklahoma process servers but is strongly recommended. Industry sources suggest $100,000-$500,000 in liability coverage and note that bonds only protect the state, not the server.
claims.source_title: Why Oklahoma Process Servers Should Carry Professional Liability Insurance
claims.quote: Most states require process servers, private investigators, and notary public officials to carry some type of a bond. Many Oklahoma inquiry agent, mobile notaries, and Oklahoma process servers might unknowingly assume that a bond covers them in all ways. This is simply not true, as bonds only serve to help protect their respective states from liability... A notary public in Oklahoma is not required to do so, but most of the major signing companies will not contact them and utilize their services unless they carry anywhere between $25,000-$100,000 of errors and omissions (E&O) insurance.

claims.statement: Industry best practices recommend documenting every service attempt with: date and time, exact address/location, description of environment, interaction details (names, descriptions, statements), weather conditions, and immediate recording after the attempt.
claims.source_title: Best Practices for Documenting Attempts to Serve Legal Papers
claims.quote: Date and Time of Each Attempt... Location of the Attempt: The exact address where the attempt occurred should be recorded... Description of the Environment... Interaction Details: Document any interactions with individuals at the location... Weather and Conditions... Record Immediately: Document service attempts as soon as they occur to ensure accuracy.

claims.statement: NAPPS was founded in 1982, is headquartered in Portland, Oregon, and represents over 2,000 members across all 50 U.S. states, the District of Columbia, seven Canadian provinces, and nearly two dozen foreign countries.
claims.source_title: NAPPS: National Association of Professional Process Servers
claims.quote: Founded in 1982 in Las Vegas NV, NAPPS today has well over 2,000 members representing all 50 states, the District of Columbia, seven Canadian provinces and nearly two dozen foreign countries. It is headquartered in Portland, Oregon.

claims.statement: Process servers should make attempts on each job within 48 hours of receipt and within every 48 hours after, per industry guidelines. Rush orders require attempts within 24 hours.
claims.source_title: ABC Legal - Attempt Reporting Requirements
claims.quote: A process server shall make attempts on each job within 48 hours of receipt and within every 48 hours after. For Rush orders attempts must be made within 24 hours of claiming the order and every 24 hours after.

claims.statement: If a process server license is revoked in Oklahoma, the licensee cannot reapply for 5 years. If revoked once, renewal fee is $1,000. A second revocation results in permanent inability to renew.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: If the license is revoked, the licensee shall not be permitted to reapply for a license for a period of five (5) years from the date of revocation. Notwithstanding any other provision of this section, any licensee whose license has been revoked one time shall pay the sum of One Thousand Dollars ($1,000.00) as a renewal fee. If a second revocation occurs, the chief or presiding judge shall not allow an applicant to renew the license.

claims.statement: License revocation proceedings may be initiated by the district attorney or Attorney General, alleging violation of law. The licensee receives at least 30 days notice by certified mail, and the hearing is before the chief or presiding judge without a jury.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: The district attorney of the county wherein a license authorized under this act has been issued or the Attorney General may file a petition in the district court to revoke the license... After at least thirty (30) days of notice by certified mail to the licensee, the chief or presiding judge, sitting without jury, shall hear the petition and enter an order thereon.

claims.statement: Upon application filing, the court clerk gives 30 days notice by posting on the county website or courthouse. Applicant must publish notice once in a legal newspaper at least 20 days before hearing. Notice is also sent to district attorney, sheriff, OSBI, and AOC.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: the court clerk shall give thirty (30) days of notice of hearing by causing the notice to be continually posted for thirty (30) days on the website of the county, or be posted in the courthouse. The applicant shall cause notice of the hearing to be made no less than twenty (20) days prior to the hearing one time by publication in a legal newspaper... The court clerk shall deliver by electronic means or deliver a copy of the notice at least twenty (20) days prior to the hearing to the district attorney, the sheriff... the Oklahoma State Bureau of Investigation and the Administrative Office of the Courts.

claims.statement: The Administrative Office of the Courts maintains a statewide registry of licensed private process servers, accessible to the public. The court clerk must also make available a list of licensed private process servers in their office.
claims.source_title: 2025 Oklahoma Statutes :: Title 12. Civil Procedure :: §12-158.1. Private process servers
claims.quote: The court clerk shall make available at all times in the office of the court clerk the list of licensed private process servers. Any person in need of the services of a process server may designate one from the names on the list... No later than January 1, 2013, the Administrative Office of the Courts shall establish and maintain a statewide registry which shall contain a list of licensed private process servers.

claims.statement: Oklahoma process servers can serve various legal documents including: subpoenas, small claims affidavits, evictions, petitions for replevin, indirect contempt citations, victim protection orders, hearings on assets, property foreclosures, temporary restraining orders, civil rights lawsuits, car accident lawsuits, child custody matters, and divorce papers.
claims.source_title: Process Server in Tulsa, Oklahoma FAQs
claims.quote: The types of papers that a process server in Norman, OK and a private detective near me at a private investigation agency near me often serves the following types of court and other legal documents: Subpoenas, Small Claims Affidavits and Orders, Evictions, 5-Day Notices to Pay or Quit, Petitions for Replevin, Indirect Contempt Citations, Victim Protection Orders (VPOs), Hearings on Assets (HoAs), Property Foreclosures, Temporary Restraining Orders (TROs), Civil Rights Lawsuits, Car Accident/Car Injury Lawsuits, Child Custody Matters, Divorce Packets, Demand Letters

claims.statement: Process servers may not serve inmates in Department of Corrections facilities directly. Service must be made to the warden or superintendent, who then delivers the documents to the inmate. The warden must reject service if the inmate is not actually present.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma Statutes §12-2004 (2025) - Process
claims.quote: upon an inmate incarcerated in an institution under the jurisdiction and control of the Department of Corrections, by delivering a copy of the summons and of the petition to the warden or superintendent or the designee of the warden or superintendent... It shall be the duty of the receiving warden or superintendent or a designee to promptly deliver the summons and petition to the inmate named therein. The warden or superintendent or his or her designee shall reject service of process for any inmate who is not actually present in the institution.

claims.statement: No specific Oklahoma state-level process server association (independent from NAPPS) was identified during research. The primary professional organization for Oklahoma process servers is NAPPS, which has multiple members in the Tulsa and Oklahoma City areas.
claims.source_title: Oklahoma State-Level Process Server Associations
claims.quote: No independent Oklahoma state-level process server association website or organization was found. NAPPS serves as the primary national organization with Oklahoma members. Oklahoma Judicial Process Servers (oklahomajudicialprocessservers.com) appears to be a private business, not a trade association.

claims.statement: Skip tracing is a service offered by professional process servers and NAPPS members. It must be conducted within legal bounds under applicable federal and state laws including the Fair Credit Reporting Act and Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act.
claims.source_title: NAPPS Member Profile - Joseph Iannazzi (Services Listed)
claims.quote: Services: PS CF CRS PC ST (Fee in advance) E-Filing, Courier [ST = Skip Tracing per NAPPS key]

claims.statement: Oklahoma has 77 counties. County court directory information sourced from OSCN.
claims.source_title: OSCN Oklahoma Court Directory
claims.quote: Directory contains 77 Oklahoma counties with court clerk information.
